```{r h1b-plot-cond}
  geom_point(data = md, aes(x = seq.fp, y = tct, color = treatment, alpha = 0.4)) +
  labs(title = "Predicted TCT across Task Iterations by Treatment", x = "Task Iteration (seq.fp)", y = "Predicted TCT") +
  facet_wrap(~ treatment) +
  theme_minimal()
```

model is overfitting / extrapolating beyond data
obviously TCT should not rapidly increase after 3-6 iterations of the task
BUT - model performance is only a measure of residuals (y error) of predicted vs real data, so the up trend only matters where there is existing data - min impact on AR, max on PWI

sufficient evidence for now to go with this model, understanding its limitations, but let's try other things - limits of model fit undermine model comparisons since we're comparing models of the data now, not the data itself

further attempts to address this with:

- explored inverse, cubic, logarithmic, logistic, and trigonometric terms as alternative to quadratic / spline in the random slopes; random intercepts LME
- attempted a non-linear mixed effects model based on exponential decay
- fit the model with valid subsets of data with balanced iterations, e.g. the first n observations for all participants that completed that many tasks

no success - worse results, convergence problems

but important to get good model fit as that is what we are comparing now, not the data itself - another layer of abstraction

continuing efforts with spline, bayesian methods moved to `h1b_mod_select.qmd`

::: content-hidden

#### Old Material

This formulation captures individual variability by allowing each participant to have a different initial TCT (intercept). It accounts for variations in TCT due to differences between participants (random effects) and within each participant's repeated measures (residuals). This design naturally incorporates all sources of participant-level variation, including imbalances due to the differing number of tasks completed by each participant, thereby addressing the data's inherent non-independence.

Variations of this model were fit using the residual maximum likelihood method (REML), as implemented by the `lmer` function from the `{lme4}` package. To address issues related to the non-linear nature of TCT, which follows an exponential decay pattern commonly observed in learning outcomes, models were fit to response variables transformed by log, square-root, and inverse (i.e., $1/\text{tct}$) functions. The results were then compared using the `compare_performance` function from the `{performance}` package. @tbl-h1b-results summarizes the results.

|Metric         |      `log(tct)` |     `sqrt(tct)` |  `inverse(tct)` |
|:--------------|:---------------:|:---------------:|:---------------:|
|R2 (cond.)     |            0.72 |            0.70 |            0.71 |
|R2 (marg.)     |            0.39 |            0.36 |            0.39 |
|ICC            |            0.54 |            0.53 |            0.52 |
|RMSE           |            0.20 |            1.03 |        2.32e-03 |
|Sigma          |            0.22 |            1.13 |        2.54e-03 |

: Comparison of Model Performance Metrics {#tbl-h1b-results .striped}

The values of ICC and $R^2$ (both conditional and marginal) are comparable across the three transformations. However, the inverse response resulted in substantially lower root mean square error (RMSE) and Sigma ($\sigma$), which represent the average and standard deviation of the residuals, respectively. With prediction errors that are both lower overall and have less variance, the inverse-transformed response, shown in @fig-inverse-tct, results in the best model performance.

```{r fig-inverse-tct}
#| fig-cap: Inverse transformation of the response
#| fig-height: 3.5
#| warning: false

# Create a data frame with the original and inverse transformed TCT values
pd <- learn_h1b |>
  mutate(inverse_tct = 1 / tct)

# Plot for tct
plot_tct <- ggplot(pd, aes(x = seq, y = tct, color = treatment)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.4) +
  geom_smooth(method = "loess", se = FALSE) +
  labs(
    title = "TCT vs. Sequence",
    x = element_blank(),
    y = element_blank()
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 10) +
  theme(
    plot.title.position = "plot",
    plot.title = element_text(size = 9, face = "bold"),
    legend.position = "none",
    axis.text.x = element_blank(),
    axis.ticks.x = element_blank(),
    # axis.text.x = element_text(margin = margin(b = 3, unit = "pt"))
  )

# Plot for inverse(tct)
plot_inverse_tct <- ggplot(pd, aes(x = seq, y = inverse_tct, color = treatment)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.4) +
  geom_smooth(method = "loess", se = FALSE) +
  labs(
    title = "Inverse(TCT) vs. Sequence",
    x = element_blank(),
    y = element_blank()
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 10) +
  theme(
    plot.title.position = "plot",
    plot.title = element_text(size = 9, face = "bold"),
    legend.position = "none",
    axis.text.x = element_blank(),
    axis.ticks.x = element_blank(),
    # axis.text.x = element_text(margin = margin(b = 3, unit = "pt"))
  )

# Display the plots
plot_tct + plot_inverse_tct +
  plot_annotation(
    title = "Task Completion Time and Inverse",
    subtitle = "Inverse transformation of TCT gives best model performance"
  )
```

:::

#### Check Model Assumptions

#### Analysis

#### $H_{1b}$ Results


### $H_{1c}$: Average Error Count per Car

```{r prep-h1c-data}
# create data
learn_h1c <- learn |>
  filter(participant != 1063) |>
  rename(tct = car_elapsed_time_adj) |>
  group_by(participant) |>
  mutate(
    seq = row_number(),
    treatment = factor(as.character(treatment))
  ) |>
  summarize(
    treatment = first(treatment),
    avg_uce = mean(uce, na.rm = T)
  )

glimpse(learn_h1c)
```

The third and final hypothesis of the learning phase:
$$H_{1b}\textrm{: Average error count per car varies with treatment}$$ 

tests a different aspect of learning. Lower error rates signal a higher quality of learning outcomes. Combined with the previous measures of efficiency (TCT) and learning rate (change in TCT per task), it provides a balanced view of training performance.

#### Applicable Statistical Methods

as with TCT, UCE data is unbalanced, not independent, probably not normally distributed; additionally, it is not continuous data, but counted values. all of these have implications for the statistical method used

however, none of this invalidates non-parametric statistical tests of aggregated (average UCE per car) data, which addresses the independence issue

#### Analysis

KW non-parametric test conducted, as seen in @fig-h1c-plot.

```{r fig-h1c-plot}
#| fig-cap: Differences in Average Uncorrected Error per Task by Treatment
#| fig-height: 5
#| warning: false

# since ggstatsplot computes confidence intervals for the effect sizes
# using bootstrapping, it is important to set a seed for reproducibility
set.seed(42)

ggstatsplot::ggbetweenstats(
  data = learn_h1c,
  x = treatment,
  y = avg_uce,
  type = "nonparametric",
  xlab = "",
  ylab = "",
  title = "Avg UCE per Task by Treatment: Kruskal-Wallis non-parametric rank sum test",
  results.subtitle = FALSE,
  subtitle = "Significant Differences Identified between PWI and others using Dunn's Test, Holm-adjusted post-hoc",
  digits = 2,
  # centrality.label.args = list(point.padding = 30),
  ggtheme = theme_minimal(base_size = 10) +
    theme(
      plot.title.position = "plot",
      plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"),
      axis.text.y.right = element_blank(),
      axis.ticks.y.right = element_blank(),
      axis.text.x = element_text(color = "black")
      # legend.position = "none",
      # axis.text.y = element_blank(),
      # axis.ticks.y = element_blank(),
    ),
  # remove side label
  ggplot.component = list(
      ggplot2::scale_y_continuous(sec.axis = ggplot2::dup_axis(name = NULL))
    )
)

# list(chi["Kruskal-Wallis"]^2 * "(" * 3 * ")" == "25.5", italic(p) == 
#     "1.2e-05", widehat(epsilon)["ordinal"]^2 == "0.5", CI["95%"] ~ 
#     "[" * "0.3", "1.0" * "]", italic("n")["obs"] == "53")
```

Results confirm significant treatment effect. Large differences in the overall mean are observed, along with some floor effects in the augmented treatments. Post-hoc tests confirm the significance of observed differences, showing that the error rate is significantly larger in the PWI condition than in all other treatments.

```{r h1c-validation}
co <- check_outliers(learn_h1c$avg_uce, method = c("zscore_robust", "iqr"))

# use co as boolean mask to get relevant participants
co_res <- learn_h1c[as.vector(co), ]

# tabulates the number of tasks completed by outlier participants
car_counts <- learn |>
  filter(participant %in% co_res$participant) |> 
  group_by(participant) |> 
  summarize(count = n()
)

# col_to_string(co_res, participant)
# col_to_string(co_res, avg_uce, digits = 1)

# check test results without outliers
without_outliers <- learn_h1c[!learn_h1c$participant %in% co_res$participant, ]
kt_h1b_no_outliers <- kruskal.test(avg_uce ~ treatment, data = without_outliers)
```

KW is a robust test that does not assume normality or homogeneity of variances. It can be sensitive to the presence of outliers, three of which were detected in the UCE data based on a combination of IQR and Z-score. Participants `r col_to_string(co_res, participant)` were all assigned to the PWI treatment and averaged `r col_to_string(co_res, avg_uce, digits = 1)` errors per task, respectively. A second Kruskal-Wallis test of the data without those outliers showed the difference remained significant (p=`r fmt_p_val(kt_h1b_no_outliers$p.val)`). Based on this it is determined that the outliers are not impacting the test result.

While this method was deemed insufficient for capturing the nuances of learning rate analysis, it is appropriate for comparing average error counts.

#### $H_{1c}$ Results

$H_{1c}$ is accepted - all augmented instruction methods result in error rates that are drastically lower in magnitude than PWI, the statistical significance of which is confirmed. 
note the floor effects

further analysis using a generalized linear mixed model with poisson or negative binomial distribution for count data could be used to better account for participant variation but that seems unnecessary given the strong differences observed here

::: content-hidden
MAYBE: learning add. analysis

### Additional Analyses

- number of cars completed - to paint a complete picture of individual performance, e.g. 1056 completed 7 cars with an average of 11.3 errors on each - inverse correlation between TCT and UCE?
- error rate of change?!
- balanced scorecard of efficiency, learning rate, quality?
- what treatment group had the highest percentage of "qualified operators" at the end of the 10-min session?

## Exploratory Analyses

### Relationship between TLX, SUS, and Performance

- first task performance in learning as a measure of initial comprehension, intuitiveness of each treatment, effectiveness of instructional design, etc.; may correlate with SUS / TLX

:::

## Challenges and Unexpected Observations {#sec-res-challenges}

Discussion of any challenges encountered during the conduct of the protocol  Modifications made to the protocol in response to these challenges

demoing AR/MR: The challenge of this choreography was compounded by significant latency in streamed video, which lagged the live action by approximately 2 seconds. With practice

> Technical Issues with Equipment: Discussion of any technical challenges encountered with the HL2 or other devices, and how these were addressed.
>
> Participant Scheduling and No-Shows: Challenges related to participant scheduling, no-shows, or last-minute cancellations, and the strategies developed to mitigate these issues.
>
> Data Collection Consistency: Any issues encountered in maintaining consistency in data collection across different trials and days, and how these were resolved.

limitations

- participant behavior
    -   few participants assigned HMDMR took advantage of the affordance
- analysis
    -   does not account for interactions between affordances

noticed improvising - discourage as occurs

sleeves, long hair problems for HL2

height and "down-lookers"; tracking dropouts;

battery life and performance; brightness and accuracy;

complexity of MR, less reliable tracking, crashes

## Summary of Key Findings
